topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright was departing. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I reread the terse announcement three times, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. I stared at the screen, the glow reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes. All the late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee, the painstaking revisions, the shared intellectual sparring sessions—it all seemed to dissolve into a sudden, vast emptiness. My hands started to tremble, and I had to grip the edge of my desk to keep from shaking them entirely. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat, a pathetic little hitch of breath. I leaned back in my chair, the worn fabric digging into my spine, and just let the silence of the empty office wash over me.

[story 2]

I found myself pacing the length of my small apartment, the worn floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. Every corner of the room felt heavy, saturated with the impending change. When I finally sat down on the sofa, I didn't bother with the textbook open on my lap. Instead, I picked up my phone, scrolling through old correspondence, re-reading the encouraging notes he’d left tucked into my drafts. A wave of profound deflation washed over me, leaving a strange, heavy lethargy in its wake. My shoulders slumped forward, the tension draining out of my neck with a noticeable slackness. I sighed, a long, shuddering exhalation that seemed to carry the weight of months of shared academic pressure.

[story 3]

The departmental gathering was a study in forced cheerfulness. Everyone offered hollow congratulations, their smiles stretched too wide. I managed a polite nod, my gaze fixed on the decorative potted plant in the corner, its vibrant green seeming mocking in its resilience. Internally, a deep ache settled in my chest, a dull, persistent throb. When a colleague asked how I was doing, I offered a tight, practiced smile that didn't reach my eyes. "Fine," I managed, the word sounding thin and brittle. I focused intently on the condensation tracing paths down my water glass, trying desperately to anchor myself to something tangible, anything other than the sudden, unsettling drift of my academic foundation.
